summary:
The Channel Tunnel, connecting Folkestone in England to Coquelles near Calais in France beneath the English Channel, opened for passenger service in November 1994 after five years of construction. The tunnel consists of three bores — two rail tunnels and a central service tunnel — running for 50.5 kilometers, of which 38 kilometers lie beneath the sea floor, making it the longest undersea tunnel in the world at the time. The project was a privately financed joint Anglo-French venture that cost approximately £9 billion, nearly double its original estimates.

The tunnel carries Eurostar passenger trains connecting London to Paris and Brussels, as well as the Eurotunnel Le Shuttle service transporting vehicles and their passengers on flatbed rail cars. Journey time from London to Paris was reduced to around two and a half hours, shorter than flying when airport transit time was included. The tunnel had been proposed in various forms since the early nineteenth century, and its construction represented both a feat of engineering and a powerful symbol of European integration at a moment when the [[event:115|European Union]] was deepening its political and economic bonds.
